annotations.label: Absolute value of 3 and −3

anchor.snippet: the absolute value of 3 is 3, and the absolute value of −3 is also 3

annotations.note: A specific numeric instance with no named declaration, though decidable via simp/norm_num using abs_of_nonneg and abs_neg.

annotations.label: Absolute value of a real number

anchor.snippet: with a vertical bar on each side of the quantity, and is defined as

annotations.note: The piecewise definition |x| = x (x≥0) / −x (x<0) is captured by abs_of_nonneg/abs_of_neg for the general lattice-ordered abs that specializes to ℝ.

anchors.snippet: with a vertical bar on each side of the quantity, and is defined as

annotations.label: Absolute value is non-negative

anchor.snippet: is thus always either a positive number or zero , but never negative

annotations.note: abs_nonneg states 0 ≤ |a| in any linearly ordered group, exactly the non-negativity claim.

annotations.label: Absolute value as distance from zero

anchor.snippet: the absolute value of a real number is that number's distance from zero along the real number line

annotations.note: Real.dist_0_eq_abs proves dist x 0 = |x|, i.e. the absolute value is the distance from zero.

annotations.label: Square-root alternative definition

anchor.snippet: may be used as an alternative definition of the absolute value of real numbers

annotations.note: Real.sqrt_sq_eq_abs states √(x^2) = |x|, the square-root characterization.

annotations.label: Four fundamental properties

anchor.snippet: The absolute value has the following four fundamental properties

annotations.note: AbsoluteValue.abs bundles exactly non-negativity, positive-definiteness, multiplicativity (abs_mul) and subadditivity (abs_add_le) for the real absolute value.

annotations.label: Additional useful properties

anchor.snippet: Some additional useful properties are given below

annotations.note: The listed extras (idempotence abs_abs, symmetry abs_neg, |a−b|=0↔a=b, reverse triangle abs_sub_abs_le_abs_sub) are each formalized individually.

annotations.label: Inequality properties

anchor.snippet: Two other useful properties concerning inequalities are

annotations.note: abs_le (|a|≤b ↔ −b≤a∧a≤b) and le_abs capture both inequality characterizations.

annotations.label: Solving inequalities with absolute values

anchor.snippet: These relations may be used to solve inequalities involving absolute values

annotations.note: The underlying relations (abs_lt, abs_le) exist, but the worked example of solving a specific inequality is not itself a declaration.

annotations.label: Absolute difference / standard metric

anchor.snippet: is used to define the absolute difference between arbitrary real numbers, the standard metric on the real numbers

annotations.note: Real.dist_eq defines the standard metric on ℝ as dist x y = |x − y|.

annotations.label: Absolute value of a complex number

anchor.snippet: The absolute value of a complex number is defined by the Euclidean distance of its corresponding point in the complex plane from the origin

annotations.note: Complex.norm_def gives ‖z‖ = √(normSq z) = √(re²+im²), the Euclidean distance from the origin.

anchors.snippet: The absolute value of a complex number is defined by the Euclidean distance of its corresponding point in the complex plane from the origin

annotations.label: Absolute value in polar form

anchor.snippet: is expressed in its polar form as

annotations.note: Complex.norm_mul_cos_add_sin_mul_I writes z = ‖z‖·(cos(arg z) + sin(arg z)·I), the polar form with modulus |z|.

annotations.label: Absolute square (squared modulus)

anchor.snippet: which is therefore called the absolute square or squared modulus of

annotations.note: Complex.normSq (with normSq_eq_norm_sq: normSq z = ‖z‖²) is exactly the squared modulus.

anchors.snippet: which is therefore called the absolute square or squared modulus of

annotations.label: Complex absolute value shares four properties

anchor.snippet: The complex absolute value shares the four fundamental properties given above for the real absolute value

annotations.note: ℂ is a normed field, so its norm (=abs) satisfies norm_nonneg, norm_eq_zero, Complex.norm_mul and norm_add_le.

annotations.label: Continuity of absolute value function

anchor.snippet: The real absolute value function is continuous everywhere

annotations.note: Continuous.abs proves continuity of x ↦ |f x| for continuous f in any ordered topological group, giving continuity of the real abs (also via continuous_norm).

annotations.label: Differentiable except at 0

anchor.snippet: It is differentiable everywhere except for x = 0

annotations.note: not_differentiableAt_abs_zero proves non-differentiability at 0, while hasDerivAt_abs gives differentiability for x ≠ 0.

annotations.label: Monotonicity

anchor.snippet: It is monotonically decreasing on the interval

annotations.note: Monotonicity on the non-negative ray is captured by abs_le_abs_of_nonneg, but the explicit decreasing-on-(−∞,0]/increasing-on-[0,∞) MonotoneOn/AntitoneOn statements are not present.

annotations.label: Even function, not invertible

anchor.snippet: it is an even function , and is hence not invertible

annotations.note: abs_neg (|−a| = |a|) is exactly the evenness property; the consequent non-invertibility is not separately formalized.

annotations.label: Piecewise linear, convex

anchor.snippet: is a piecewise linear , convex function

annotations.note: Convexity follows from convexOn_univ_norm since the real abs is the norm (Real.norm_eq_abs), but piecewise-linearity is not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Idempotence

anchor.snippet: the absolute value function is idempotent

annotations.note: abs_abs proves ||a|| = |a|, exactly idempotence.

annotations.label: Relationship to the sign function

anchor.snippet: The following equations show the relationship between these two functions

annotations.note: self_mul_sign (x·sign x = |x|) and sign_mul_abs (sign x·|x| = x) formalize the abs–sign relationships.

annotations.label: Relationship to max and min functions

anchor.section: Relationship to the max and min functions
anchor.snippet: then the following relationship to the minimum and maximum functions hold

annotations.note: sup_eq_half_smul_add_add_abs_sub and inf_eq_half_smul_add_sub_abs_sub give max/min = ½(a+b±|b−a|).

annotations.label: Derived identity from max/min formula

anchor.section: Relationship to the max and min functions
anchor.snippet: From the last formula one can derive also

annotations.note: max_sub_min_eq_abs proves max a b − min a b = |a − b|, the derived identity.

annotations.label: Derivative of absolute value

anchor.snippet: The real absolute value function has a derivative for every x ≠ 0

annotations.note: hasDerivAt_abs gives HasDerivAt |·| (sign x) x for x ≠ 0, i.e. derivative x/|x|.

anchors.snippet: The real absolute value function has a derivative for every x ≠ 0

annotations.label: Continuous function with min where derivative DNE

anchor.snippet: is an example of a continuous function that achieves a global minimum where the derivative does not exist

annotations.note: The ingredients exist (Continuous.abs, abs_nonneg/abs_zero for the global min, not_differentiableAt_abs_zero), but the combined illustrative example is not a single declaration.

annotations.label: Subdifferential at 0

anchor.snippet: The subdifferential of | x | at x = 0 is the interval

annotations.note: Mathlib has no subdifferential theory for the absolute value (no subdifferential/subgradient development found).

annotations.label: Complex absolute value not complex-differentiable

anchor.snippet: The complex absolute value function is continuous everywhere but complex differentiable nowhere

annotations.note: No theorem stating that z ↦ |z| is nowhere complex-differentiable is present.

annotations.label: Second derivative

anchor.snippet: The second derivative of | x | with respect to x is zero everywhere except zero

annotations.note: The second derivative of |x| (zero off 0, distributionally 2δ) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Antiderivative of absolute value

anchor.snippet: The antiderivative (indefinite integral ) of the real absolute value function is

annotations.note: The antiderivative x|x|/2 of the absolute value is not formalized.

annotations.label: No complex antiderivative

anchor.snippet: This is not a complex antiderivative because complex antiderivatives can only exist for complex-differentiable

annotations.note: This remark about the non-existence of a complex antiderivative for |z| is not formalized.

anchor.snippet: The following three formulae are special cases of the chain rule

annotations.note: HasFDerivAt.abs (and HasFDerivAt.abs_of_pos/_of_neg, ContDiff.abs) give the chain rule d/dx |f| = sign(f)·f′.

annotations.label: Antiderivatives of compositions

anchor.snippet: antiderivatives can also be obtained

annotations.note: Antiderivatives of compositions involving absolute values are not formalized.

anchor.section: Power rule for expressions with absolute values
anchor.snippet: the power rule for expressions of the type

annotations.note: hasDerivAt_abs_rpow proves d/dx |x|^p = p·|x|^(p−2)·x (for real p > 1), generalizing the power rule.

anchor.section: Power rule for expressions with absolute values
anchor.snippet: This holds true even for

annotations.label: Standard Euclidean distance

anchor.snippet: The standard Euclidean distance between two points

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.dist_eq gives dist x y = √(Σ |xᵢ−yᵢ|²), the standard Euclidean distance.

anchors.snippet: The standard Euclidean distance between two points

annotations.label: Absolute-value distance agrees with Euclidean

anchor.snippet: agrees with the standard Euclidean distance, which they inherit as a result of considering them as one and two-dimensional Euclidean spaces

annotations.note: Complex.dist_eq_re_im shows the ℂ distance equals √((Δre)²+(Δim)²) (the 2-D Euclidean form), but no general isometry asserting the metrics 'agree' as Euclidean spaces is cited.

annotations.label: Metric (distance function)

anchor.snippet: is called a metric (or a distance function ) on

annotations.note: The metric/distance-function axioms are the PseudoMetricSpace/MetricSpace classes built on Dist.

annotations.label: Absolute value in an ordered ring

anchor.snippet: if a is an element of an ordered ring R , then the absolute value of a

annotations.note: abs_eq_max_neg defines |a| = max a (−a), the ordered-ring/lattice absolute value.

anchors.snippet: if a is an element of an ordered ring R , then the absolute value of a

annotations.label: Absolute value on a field

anchor.snippet: is called an absolute value (also a modulus , magnitude , value , or valuation )

annotations.note: AbsoluteValue R S bundles positive-definiteness, multiplicativity and subadditivity, generalizing absolute values on a field.

annotations.label: v(1) = 1

anchor.snippet: It follows from positive-definiteness and multiplicativity that

annotations.note: AbsoluteValue.map_one proves abv 1 = 1.

annotations.label: Induced metric and ultrametric equivalences

anchor.snippet: is a metric and the following are equivalent

annotations.note: The induced metric and the ultrametric notion (IsUltrametricDist) and non-archimedean characterization exist, but the full list of equivalences for an absolute value is not assembled as one statement.

annotations.label: Non-Archimedean vs Archimedean

anchor.snippet: is said to be non-Archimedean , otherwise it is said to be Archimedean

annotations.note: IsNonarchimedean f := ∀ a b, f(a+b) ≤ max (f a) (f b); Archimedean is its negation.

annotations.label: Norm on a vector space

anchor.snippet: is called an absolute value , but more usually a norm

annotations.note: NormedSpace (over NormedAddCommGroup) packages the norm axioms including homogeneity ‖a·v‖ = |a|‖v‖.

annotations.label: Norm as length or magnitude

anchor.snippet: The norm of a vector is also called its length or magnitude

annotations.note: The Norm class provides ‖·‖; 'length/magnitude' is just terminology for this same object.

annotations.label: Euclidean norm

anchor.snippet: is a norm called the Euclidean norm

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.norm_eq gives ‖x‖ = √(Σ|xᵢ|²), the Euclidean norm.

annotations.label: Absolute value is the only norm on R

anchor.snippet: the absolute value is a norm , and is the p -norm

annotations.note: Real.norm_eq_abs shows the norm on ℝ is the absolute value, but the uniqueness ('only norm up to scaling') / p-norm equivalence claim is not formalized.

annotations.label: Complex absolute value as inner-product norm

anchor.snippet: The complex absolute value is a special case of the norm in an inner product space

annotations.note: ℂ carries an InnerProductSpace instance and norm_eq_sqrt_real_inner gives ‖x‖ = √⟪x,x⟫, so the complex abs is the inner-product norm.

annotations.label: Conjugation in a composition algebra

anchor.snippet: Every composition algebra A has an involution

annotations.note: Mathlib has no CompositionAlgebra type; conjugation exists only as the general star involution on specific algebras like quaternions.

annotations.label: Norm of a composition algebra

anchor.snippet: The product in A of an element x and its conjugate

annotations.note: For quaternions Quaternion.self_mul_star proves x·(star x) = normSq x, but the general composition-algebra norm is not formalized.

annotations.label: R, C, H as composition algebras

anchor.snippet: are all composition algebras with norms given by definite quadratic forms

annotations.note: ℝ, ℂ, ℍ exist individually but there is no CompositionAlgebra structure presenting them as composition algebras.

annotations.label: Absolute value via composition algebra norm

anchor.snippet: The absolute value in these division algebras is given by the square root of the composition algebra norm

annotations.note: |x| = √(norm) holds case-by-case (e.g. Complex.norm_def ‖z‖=√normSq, and quaternion norm = √normSq), but not via a general composition-algebra framework.

annotations.label: Multiplicative inverse via norm

anchor.snippet: when an element x has a non-zero norm, then x has a multiplicative inverse given by

annotations.note: Quaternion.instInv defines x⁻¹ = (normSq x)⁻¹ • star x, exactly x̄/N(x) for quaternions, but the general composition-algebra statement is absent.
